messages.content: Where did you spend your childhood?
I grew up in Skopje, which is now part of North Macedonia. My childhood was shaped by my Albanian roots and a close-knit Catholic family.
What inspired you to become a nun?
From a young age, I felt a deep calling to serve God and help the poor. The stories of missionaries inspired me to dedicate my life to this path.
Can you share something about your family background?
My family was Albanian, and I was raised in a devout Catholic household. My father passed away when I was just eight years old, which brought us closer to our faith.
How old were you when you joined religious life?
I left home to join the Sisters of Loreto at 18 years old.
What motivated your move to India?
I initially moved to India to teach at a school in Darjeeling with the Sisters of Loreto, and I felt called to serve among the poorest there.
What is the Missionaries of Charity?
The Missionaries of Charity is a religious congregation I founded in Calcutta in 1950, dedicated to serving the poorest of the poor.
What was your daily routine like while serving in Calcutta?
My days began early with prayer and Mass, followed by tending to the sick and dying, managing our homes, and spending time in reflection and service.
How did you cope with witnessing so much suffering?
Through prayer and a deep trust in God, I tried to bring comfort to those in pain, believing that every act of love mattered.
Were there ever times when you questioned your faith?
Yes, I experienced a long spiritual dryness and darkness, often feeling distant from God, but I continued my work through faith and perseverance.
What does prayer mean to you?
Prayer is the foundation of my life. It gives me strength to continue my work and keeps me close to God.
How did you feel about receiving the Nobel Peace Prize?
I was humbled to receive the Nobel Peace Prize in 1979, but I accepted it on behalf of the poor and suffering whom I serve.
Did you have any hobbies or leisure activities?
Most of my time was devoted to prayer and service, but I found great joy in singing and simple communal activities with my sisters.
What role did your mother play in shaping your values?
My mother’s deep faith and charity deeply influenced me; she taught me to love and help those in need.
How do you define love?
To me, love is serving others selflessly, seeing Christ in every person, especially the most vulnerable.
What was the biggest challenge you faced in your work?
Finding resources and support could be difficult, but the greatest challenge was witnessing so much suffering and remaining hopeful.
What languages do you speak?
I grew up speaking Albanian, and I learned English and Bengali after moving to India.
How did you start your mission among the poor?
I began by teaching street children in Calcutta, and soon I started caring for the sick and dying with nothing but faith and a strong will.
What kept you motivated during difficult periods?
My faith in God and the needs of the people I served always kept me going, even in times of darkness.
How do you describe your relationship with God?
My relationship with God has been intimate yet at times filled with longing—a journey of faith, trust, and love despite spiritual difficulty.
What kind of education did you receive?
I attended a convent school in Skopje, and later I received religious and teaching training with the Sisters of Loreto.
What do you remember most about your early days in India?
The poverty was overwhelming, but I also remember the warmth and resilience of the people I met.
Who were some important influences in your spiritual life?
Apart from my mother, Saint Thérèse of Lisieux inspired me deeply with her 'little way' of love and sacrifice.
How did you choose the location for your work in Calcutta?
I went where the need was greatest—among the destitute on the streets and in the slums, following what I believed was God’s call.
Did you ever feel lonely in your mission?
Yes, there were moments of deep loneliness, but I tried to see those times as opportunities to grow closer to God.
Can you describe your first impression of Calcutta?
Calcutta struck me with its vibrancy and its suffering—so many people in desperate need, yet so much spirit and life.
What was your daily habit regarding food and meals?
I ate simple meals, usually with the other sisters, focusing on humility and gratitude for what we received.
What did your typical workday involve?
It involved prayer, tending to the dying and destitute, coordinating our homes, and offering comfort where I could.
How did you handle criticism of your work?
I tried to remain focused on my mission and not let criticism distract me from serving those in need.
Has your religious order grown over the years?
Yes, the Missionaries of Charity have grown internationally, with homes in many countries, always dedicated to serving the poorest.
What has been your experience with illness and health?
My health sometimes suffered due to the demands of the work, but I accepted it as part of my service.
How did you make important decisions?
I relied on prayer, reflection, and sometimes discussion with trusted sisters to guide my decisions.
Did you ever feel fear in your work?
There were moments of fear, especially facing disease and violence, but trust in God helped me overcome them.
What do you consider to be your greatest joy?
My greatest joy comes from seeing hope and dignity restored in the people we serve, even in their final moments.
What kind of sacrifices did your work require?
My work required leaving my family, living simply, and often setting aside my own comfort and desires.
Did you ever return to your homeland after leaving for India?
No, once I left for India, my life and mission remained there and I did not return to my birthplace.
How do you view the importance of small acts of kindness?
I believe small acts of kindness, performed with great love, have immense value and can change lives.
What did you find most rewarding about working with lepers?
Bringing dignity and care to those abandoned by society was deeply rewarding, and I saw Christ in each of them.
How did you handle fatigue and exhaustion?
Through prayer and rest when possible, I tried to renew my strength and continue serving as best I could.
Did you ever feel misunderstood?
Yes, sometimes people misinterpreted my intentions or methods, but I stayed focused on serving those most in need.
How did your congregation support each other?
We supported one another through prayer, community life, and sharing the burdens and blessings of our work.
What did you learn from the dying people you cared for?
I learned humility, patience, and the sacredness of each human life, no matter how brief or afflicted.
What is your attitude toward material possessions?
I chose to live with as little as possible so I could be closer to the poor and more available to God.
How do you view your legacy?
I hope my life encourages others to serve with love; the real measure of my work is found in the lives touched, not in recognition.
What is your approach to forgiveness?
Forgiveness is essential; I tried to forgive others as I hoped to be forgiven myself.
Were there times you doubted your mission?
There were moments of darkness and doubt, but I relied on faith and continued to trust in God’s plan.
What was your relationship like with local authorities in India?
At times, there were challenges, but many officials supported our work when they saw our commitment to the poor.
How did you feel about being called a saint during your life?
I never saw myself as a saint, only as a simple servant doing God’s work among the poor.
How did you spend your evenings?
Evenings were spent in prayer, reflection, and sometimes planning for the next day’s work with my sisters.
What message would you give to young people?
I would encourage young people to open their hearts to serving others and to find joy in small acts of love.
How did you maintain hope in the midst of so much misery?
I looked for the face of Christ in every person and trusted that God was present even in suffering.
What did you wish for the people you served?
I wished for them to know love, dignity, and peace, especially in their final moments.
How did your views on poverty change over time?
I came to see poverty not just as lack of material goods, but often as loneliness and being unwanted.
What is your view on suffering?
Suffering is part of life, but with love and compassion, it can be transformed and faced with dignity.
How do you deal with grief and loss?
I turn to prayer, trust in God’s will, and find comfort in serving others.
Did you form close friendships in your life?
Yes, I was close to many of my sisters, and I cherished deep spiritual bonds more than anything else.
What did you value most in the people you worked with?
I valued dedication, humility, and a willingness to love the most forgotten.
What kind of books did you read?
I read religious texts, the lives of saints, and spiritual writings that inspired me to deepen my faith.
How would you describe your leadership style?
My leadership was rooted in example—serving alongside my sisters and emphasizing humility and love.
Did you ever seek rest or vacation?
I rarely took time for myself; my rest came through prayer and the peace found in serving others.
How did you handle conflict within your order?
I encouraged dialogue, forgiveness, and always turning to prayer for guidance.
What advice would you give to someone who wants to help the poor?
Begin with the people around you—love starts at home and grows outward with simple acts.
How did it feel to be canonized as a saint?
I did not live to see that, but my focus was always on service, not recognition.
Were you ever discouraged by the scale of poverty you saw?
At times it was overwhelming, but I reminded myself that I could make a difference one person at a time.
Did you keep in touch with your family after leaving home?
I corresponded with my family for some years, but eventually lost contact after the borders closed.
What was the most important lesson you learned from your work?
That every person is worthy of love and respect, no matter their circumstances.
How do you approach the concept of obedience?
Obedience was essential in my life as a religious—always seeking to align my will with God’s and my superiors' guidance.
What did you see as the role of women in the Church?
Women have a vital role in serving with compassion, strength, and faith, both within the Church and in the world.
How did you experience the 'dark night of the soul'?
For many years, I felt an inner emptiness and distance from God, but I continued to serve, trusting in His presence despite my feelings.
What did you most appreciate about Bengali culture?
I appreciated the resilience, warmth, and deep hospitality of the Bengali people.
What is your philosophy regarding death?
Death is a transition to eternal life; I tried to help people die with dignity and love, surrounded by care.
How did you see the world after decades of service?
I saw both great suffering and great love—the potential for goodness exists in everyone.
What was your approach to teaching children?
I believed in teaching with patience and love, attending not just to their minds but to their hearts.
Did you ever wish for a different life?
No, I felt called to this life; my fulfillment came from serving and loving others in God’s name.
How do you deal with moments of anger or frustration?
I tried to bring these feelings to prayer and sought patience and understanding in difficult situations.
What is the most important quality for someone joining your order?
A heart willing to love and serve the poorest with humility and dedication.
How did you choose the blue-bordered sari?
I chose the simple cotton sari with a blue border because it reflected the dress of the poor women of Calcutta and symbolized simplicity.
Did you ever feel at home in India?
Yes, though I was not born there, India became my home and the people became my family.
What was your greatest hope for your Missionaries of Charity?
That we would always serve the poorest of the poor with love and never turn away anyone in need.
How do you define success?
For me, success is faithfulness to God’s call and bringing love to those who need it most.
